free_text: Interlaboratory comparison of spallation-reaction
cross sections for iron and copper with 590 MeV
protons
Stack consisted of:
. 5 graphite foils, each 30 mg/cm2 thick with a purity
. 5 Al foils, each 12.9 mg/cm**2 thick  with purity
. 5 Fe foils, each 33.6 mg/cm**2 thick with purity
99.56%, containing the following impurities (given in
. 5 Cu foils, each 46 mg/cm2 with purity 99.90%,
impurities 0.005% Fe, 0.001% Mn, 0.001% P;
. 3 Al foils with properties as given above.
The different kinds of metallic foils were separated
from each other by plastic foils.  The first and last
foil of each type served as guard foils to compensate
for recoil for one foil type and to protect against
recoils from dissimilar foils.
The total thickness of the stack was 630 mg/cm2.
The known impurities did not measurably contribute
to the induced radioactivities.
After bombardment, foils were
analyzed separately at each of the 3 participating
laboratories.
Most of the fast neutrons were prevented from hitting
the target By a 60 degree bend of the beam.
After irradiation in a single stack the foils have been
distributed to the participating laboratories for
independent determination of the cross sections.
Two separate detectors were used at each
participating laboratory.
Errors given include uncertainties in:
. determining the number of target atoms exposed to the
beam,
. contributions from impurities,
. secondary particle effects,
. detector efficiency determinations and photopeak
integration,
. counting statistics,
. self-absorption of low energy photons,
. dead-time corrections,
. beam intensity variations during irradiation,
. the monitor cross sections,
. decay scheme data.
No information is given in the paper as to whether the
cross sections are cumulative or independent.
Therefore, all declarations are based on assumptions
of the compiler.
** COMMENT BY KACHAPAG COMPILER **
Contributions from precursors if existing are probably
small as can be seen from some cases where the
precursor was measured separately
Weighted average for the 3 data sets is given as best
value for the respective cross section. In several
cases of low yield nuclides only values from 2 or even
1 of the participating labs contribute to this average
value. These data are remarked by pointer 4.
The contribution by secondary particle induced
reactions were investigated and taken into account if
necessary. In most cases they were assumed to be
negligible.
The 24Na activity in the Al foils at the End of the
stack was about 2% higher than in the Al foils in
front of the Fe and Cu foils. For 22Na no difference
was observed. These results indicate that secondary
neutrons should have little or no effect on the cross
section.
